author: @RWBHere
text: So, you have a form of direct conversion of sunlight to electricity. It would be interesting to find out what the optimum conversion efficiency is, to see whether it compares favourably with solar PV cells - which also produce about 0.5 V per cell. It would also be worth knowing how much (preferably how little) they cost per Watt to make. Time for some experiments then.  Thank-you, Robert.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
How about providing some chemical equations to explain the chemistry?
A half a volt is useful but at what current?
I can see an applications in backing up solar panels, but is it better to use a capacitor or more conventional battery to provide backup when the sun is not shining? The attraction is the forever part. Even if only used to provide power for IoT devices, the fit and forget feature is very attractive. More so than having to replace the backup battery every few years.
How is this battery affected by humidity, does it die when exposed to moisture?

author: @kennybentley1161
text:
rob, I've always had a question that this video reminds me of (and hopefully you may know the answer). when I smoke a cigarette, the smoke coming off of it appears to have a blue hue to it, especially in the sunlight or the moonlight. however, when I exhale, the smoke that comes out of my lungs does not have the bluish hue to it and appears white.


is that a similar effect to what is happening in this battery?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think so mate - I think it is just particle size and moisture content

replies.author: @kennybentley1161
replies.text:
as a followup, I felt a reaction to my last line there where I said, "I hope that was of interest to you". I didn't really think much of it at the time, and I suppose I was just copying you. however, I think the reaction that I felt was relevant because in the other one we were talking about community and how streamers make that by changing vocabulary.


so what happened is I said something technically interesting like you often do and then followed it up with "I hope that was of interest to you" -- which isn't probably the best way to build a community, when I said stuff like that because it sounds like I'm the enlightened one passing out the information to the peasant surfs or something, lol. anyway, the reaction was worth noting, I think.


---


additionally, when I woke up this morning I realised that what tesla was talking about with the inefficiencies atmosphere was about increasing the oxygen levels in the air battery of the planet. he was trying to do the same thing that you're doing by increasing energy density but on the scale of the size of the planet... so, it seems to me quite likely that if some way (electrically or chemically) you could increase the amount of oxygen in your air batteries, you could increase energy density. do you think those iron blue/white colours were oxidisation reactions?


ok cheers :)

author: @eugenes9751
text: As far as I'm aware, metal-air batteries are not rechargeable. The energy comes from the metal breaking down (oxidation), so there's no way to "un-break" it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: this is not a metal air battery - i said that - this is prussian blue going to prussian white - a well known photoreaction - did you watch the video or just post first?

replies.author: @eugenes9751
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering Sorry, never heard of Prussian blue before. Seems like you're describing a sodium ion battery. That technology can not compete with lithium. Sodium is 3x heavier, you can't extract as much energy as you can with lithium, and the manufacturing costs are the same as lithium batteries. The material cost of a battery is only about 20-30% of the total cost, so even with free sodium, you would still end up with a battery that is 3x heavier, with a lower voltage, at 70-80% of the cost of lithium.... 
I also highly doubt that this bleaching process can make any meaningful electricity, try making one and compare it to the smallest solar cell. Also, this would require the prussian blue to be on the exterior of the battery.... I can imagine some serious issues with that. Finally, voltage numbers means nothing without a load.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @eugenes9751 It's fair enough you haven't heard of Prussian blue - but really - you need to read before commenting - go look on google scholar - it might help with a few misconceptions you have here - I mean that in a nice way mate - I am not being snotty here - but you do seem to have a genuine interest and to engage in a proper conversation here - you do need a few more facts - have a look at Prussian blue analogue batteries - I think it will be of interest to you

replies.author: @eugenes9751
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering I'll gladly look at the research, but why have you not cited any of it in the notes?
Would it be possible for you to do a video talking about the actual details of how it works? I get that it's a "well known reaction", but your video glanced over it as if everybody is expected to know what you're talking about. To make such a bold claim of "self charging battery" and then simply expect everyone to understand your methodology is a bit unfair.


Here is my concern: I see you make a lot of videos talking about a lot of different ideas, and ending with "this warrants more research". My question is: why don't you do the research first, and then put out the video? Are you under the impression that you're the first person to ever come up with these ideas? You've come up with a way to "self-charge" a battery, while Elon Musk is building gigafactories and not getting it?? 
I hope you understand that my cynicism comes from seeing hundreds of other "battery gugus" make claims entirely out of their ass, and never once create a manufacturable product. I'm not saying you're doing that, just that, in my opinion, you need to provide a lot more information for your claims before making such wild assumptions.

author: @RandyRandersonthefamous

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: Science or anything for that matter is 5% deisgn/build and 95% test.  You find the time, internet.  The man spent thousands of hours building up to this with knowledge, leaps of faith, and sticktuitiveness.   So, consider your question answered.  Here's a great example, I can say for certain, being in the industry, that any basic software project run by experienced staff will be 5-15% arch spec/PoC, 5% actual coding, and 80-90% test/validation.    You can bring your test cycles down using verification techniques/methods, but most don't want to invest in what it costs to actually run verification properly even thought the ROI is substantially proven.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: look at some more of the vids - also - who really watches a video of a test - i can tell you - no one - the videos i do of testing get maybe a couple of hundred views - did you watch one for example?
